MAC (minimum alveolar concentration) is the alveolar concentration preventing purposeful movement to a surgical stimulus in {{c1::50%}} of subjects, measured in O2 at {{c2::1 atmosphere}}.
MAC required for surgery without adjuncts is {{c1::1.2–1.3 MAC}}, which prevents movement in approximately {{c2::95%}} of patients.
MAC is a {{c1::spinal reflex}} endpoint located at the {{c2::dorsal horn}}, not a cortical one. Explains why opioids are MAC-sparing.
Opioids reduce MAC by {{c1::50–70%}} via mu-receptor action at the dorsal horn, blunting the nociceptive afferent arc.
An agent with a high blood:gas coefficient (high solubility) has {{c1::slow}} onset
column2: a low blood:gas coefficient produces {{c2::fast}} onset. Blood drains the alveolus when solubility is high, keeping FA low.

column1: End-tidal concentration is a reliable surrogate for brain concentration because brain tension approaches alveolar tension within {{c1::8–10 minutes}} of any change.
In a low cardiac output state, ↓ CO → ↓ alveolar drainage → {{c1::↑ FA}} → {{c2::deeper anaesthesia}} than expected at a given vaporiser setting.
column2: Reduce vaporiser setting proactively in shocked or low-CO patients.

column1: Isoflurane MAC is {{c1::1.15%}}.
Isoflurane has a {{c1::pungent}} odour and is an airway irritant
column2: it is {{c2::<u>not suitable</u>}} for inhalational induction.

column1: Isoflurane causes ↓ MAP via ↓ {{c1::SVR}}, with cardiac output {{c2::relatively preserved}} and minimal myocardial depressant effect.
Isoflurane ↓ {{c1::cerebral metabolic rate}} with minimal change in CBF or ICP, making it useful in {{c2::neurosurgery}}.
Sevoflurane MAC is {{c1::~2%}}.
Sevoflurane has a pleasant smell and minimal airway irritation — it is the {{c1::preferred agent}} for inhalational (gas) induction.
Sevoflurane inhalational induction uses {{c1::5–8%}} inspired concentration, achieving sleep in {{c2::1–2}} vital capacity breaths.
Sevoflurane ↓ BP via ↓ {{c1::SVR}} without a corresponding ↓ in {{c2::cardiac output}}.
Sevoflurane is a potent {{c1::bronchodilator}} and has been used in {{c2::refractory status asthmaticus}} requiring controlled ventilation.
Sevoflurane is metabolised to {{c1::Compound A}} by CO2 absorbers, causing potential {{c2::renal tubular toxicity}} at high concentrations or low FGF.
Desflurane MAC is {{c1::6%}}.
Desflurane has the {{c1::most rapid}} onset and offset of all volatile agents, giving the shortest time to emergence and orientation.
Desflurane is the {{c1::most pungent}} of all volatile agents and is {{c2::not suitable}} for inhalational induction.
Rapidly ↑ desflurane concentration → sympathetic activation → ↑ {{c1::HR}} and ↑ {{c2::BP}} ± myocardial ischaemia via ↑ plasma noradrenaline and adrenaline.
The 'Rule of 24' for desflurane: FGF (L/min) × inspired desflurane concentration (%) must be {{c1::< 24}} to avoid sympathetic surge.
Desflurane produces {{c1::carbon monoxide}} when exposed to {{c2::desiccated CO2 absorbers}}
column2: risk is greatest with desflurane compared with isoflurane and sevoflurane.

column1: Nitrous oxide (N2O) is an inert, {{c1::inorganic}} gas with a MAC of {{c2::104%}}, meaning it cannot produce GA alone at safe concentrations.
N2O is {{c1::34×}} more soluble in blood than nitrogen. This underpins both the second gas effect and diffusion hypoxia.
The maximum usable concentration of N2O is {{c1::70%}}, as a minimum FiO2 of {{c2::30%}} must be maintained during GA.
N2O recovery time is {{c1::3–10 minutes}} due to its {{c2::low blood solubility}}.
At 66% N2O, a closed air space expands to {{c1::2×}} its original volume over approximately {{c2::15 minutes}}.
All {{c1::halogenated volatile}} agents (isoflurane, sevoflurane, desflurane) are contraindicated in malignant hyperthermia (MH) susceptibility
Risk of CO production with desiccated CO2 absorbers, in descending order: {{c1::desflurane >> isoflurane > sevoflurane}}.
Isoflurane, sevoflurane, and desflurane all cause {{c1::dose-dependent uterine relaxation}}, which is clinically relevant in {{c2::obstetrics and uterine surgery}}.
